author: @reypolice5231
text:
There is a guy on YouTube that put a propeller on the front of his car and got 2k Watts, and he Hook it to an oven for load test.  The propeller also pushed the air around the SUV so no loss of mileage. His next step was to use power to run the SUV that takes about 1.5-2K watts to run the SUV. 

I am told it takes 15 HP to run an alternator? 

So if we took the RMS TESLA version and put that on a truck, power and exhaust it to the sided of the truck.

author: @jsmxwll
text: Whenever I've dug into the history of a technology, there is almost always direct inspiration from some seemingly inane or possibly insane inventions somewhere in that technology's lineage. Even something like the electric car was thought to be a completely useless waste due to it being tied to a power source. It took advancement in batteries to untie electric cars from the grid and make them something with potential. I remember seeing hacked together electric cars when I was a teen. They were running on marine lead acid batteries with maybe ten miles of range. Everyone thought it was insane, but it was certainly a step forward from being directly connected to the grid. Technology often converges in interesting and unexpected ways giving us things we didn't even know we wanted.

author: @MadeleineTakam_Info_on_Profile
text: A quick mention of the Canal system should be made, which transported a huge weight and quantity of materials from the late 1750’s. Although railways have been around in mining for over 4000 years. It was actually the canal system that allowed for the production of the huge quantities of steel required to make modern railways practical from late 1780’s.

author: @edwardphillips8460
text:
Not having ideas to try, would be sad and truly crazy. Some new ideas are good some not so much. I’m old enough to remember my own father saying “A computer in your home! No one in their right mind needs one of those. It just a fad, we don’t need one.”
Yet he could diagnose your car with a quick listen and be right 99.9% of the time. The .1% error was largely do to the car being a Ford. He did the 40 - 45 marching tour of Europe. Saw all the sights, England, Italy, Holland, and of course Germany.
Returned home with a we can make do mind set. He was brilliant at make do.
I’m positive there are ideas from the past that were too far ahead of the feasibility of the time, (e.g. Divinci’s Helicopter).  Yet if looked a with current knowledge and capabilities they might not be so crazy.
